summary: DeVault outlines how FOSS maintainers should actively guide contributors into roles that suit their skills and interests, similar to corporate advancement tracks. He describes the process of observing contributors' strengths, encouraging them to branch out into new tasks like code review, and fostering collaboration across modules. The ultimate goal is to gradually delegate maintainer responsibilities so the project can scale beyond any single person. He reserves conflict resolution for himself, using his position to absorb blame and preserve relationships between contributors.
key_points: FOSS projects need diverse roles beyond coding — support, design, marketing, documentation, translations — and maintainers must match people to tasks
Contributors should be encouraged to branch out, especially into code review, even at the cost of occasional bugs
The maintainer's goal is to gradually work themselves out of the machine by onboarding others into leadership tasks
Conflict resolution is a key maintainer role: making final calls absorbs blame and preserves contributor relationships
A healthy project has low bus factor, with contributors supporting each other at every level
money_quotes: Just like many companies have different advancement tracks for their employees, similar concepts exist in free software projects.
One of the roles of a maintainer is to help contributors develop into the roles which best suit them.
Don't be afraid to take risks - a few bugs is a small price to pay for an experienced contributor.
The final step is to gradually work your way out of the machine.
By making the decision, I aim to make it neither party's fault that someone didn't get their way.
Instead, I point any blame at myself, and rely on the mutual trust between myself and the contributors to see the decision through amicably.
If you're lucky, the end result is a project which can grow arbitrarily large, with contributors bringing a variety of skills to support each other at every level.
key_insight: A FOSS maintainer's most important job is not writing code but developing contributors into self-sufficient roles and gradually delegating leadership until the project no longer depends on any single person.
